---
title: "Exercise: Numbers and bits"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

A warm-up that gets the three number bases (decimal [base 10], binary [base 2], hex [base 16]) and the standard **bitwise operators** [AND, OR, XOR, shift, NOT — operations that act on every bit of an integer independently] into your fingers. You will write five tiny functions:

1. `show_three_bases(x: u8) -> String` that returns a formatted string like `"13 = 0b00001101 = 0x0d"`.
2. `bit_count(x: u32) -> u32` using [`u32::count_ones`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u32.html#method.count_ones) (gloss: hardware **popcount** [count of 1-bits], a single CPU instruction on every modern x86/ARM chip).
3. `three_ops(a: u8, b: u8) -> (u8, u8, u8)` returning the bitwise AND, OR, and XOR of two bytes.
4. `set_bit(x: u8, i: u8) -> u8` and `clear_bit(x: u8, i: u8) -> u8` for any bit position 0..8.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have used [`format!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.format.html) with the `{:b}` and `{:x}` format specifiers, the bitwise operators [`&`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.BitAnd.html), [`|`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.BitOr.html), [`^`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.BitXor.html), [`!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.Not.html), and [`<<`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.Shl.html), and the standard-library popcount [`u32::count_ones`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u32.html#method.count_ones).

## Background

A `u8` is **8 bits** of memory — a single byte, the smallest addressable unit on every modern CPU. Those 8 bits can represent any integer from 0 to 255. The same 8 bits can be *displayed* three ways:

- **Decimal** (base 10): the way humans read numbers — `13`.
- **Binary** (base 2): the way the machine stores them — `0b00001101`.
- **Hex** (base 16): a compact shorthand programmers use because each hex digit corresponds exactly to 4 binary digits (a **nibble** [half a byte]) — `0x0d`.

All three are the *same number*. Rust's [`format!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.format.html) macro can print any integer in any of these bases with the `{:b}` and `{:x}` format specifiers, and the leading-zero padding `{:08b}` says "binary, at least 8 digits, padded with 0 on the left".

The five **bitwise operators** treat their operands as bags of independent bits:

| op | name | meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `a & b` | AND | bit set where *both* a and b have it set |
| `a \| b` | OR | bit set where *either* has it set |
| `a ^ b` | XOR | bit set where exactly *one* has it set (mismatch) |
| `!a` | NOT | flip every bit |
| `a << n` | left shift | move all bits left by n positions (zero-fill on the right) |

These five operators plus **popcount** [counting the 1-bits in an integer] are the entire vocabulary of bit-twiddling. In exercise 3 you will use them to compute Hamming distance [number of positions at which two equal-length strings differ] on packed DNA in a handful of CPU instructions; in `ex-barcode-whitelist` you will use them to match single-cell barcodes against a whitelist with one mismatch tolerated.

## The exercise

Open the starter project:

```bash
cd day13/ex-numbers-and-bits
```

You will see a single Rust source file at `src/main.rs`. It already contains:

- Five functions with `// TODO` bodies: `show_three_bases`, `bit_count`, `three_ops`, `set_bit`, `clear_bit`
- A `main` that prints a small demo of all five
- A `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block with six unit tests that pin down the expected behaviour

Your job: replace the five `TODO` bodies so that all six tests pass.

You may **not** change the function signatures, the tests, or `main`. You may add helper functions.

## How to run

From inside `day13/ex-numbers-and-bits/`:

```bash
cargo build           # compile (no dependencies, so this is instant)
cargo test            # run the tests - these should be failing at first
cargo run             # print the demo
```

When all six tests pass, `cargo test` will print:

```
test result: ok. 6 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 - How do I format binary and hex?"}
Rust's [`format!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.format.html) macro takes the same format specifiers as `println!`. The two you need are `{:b}` (binary) and `{:x}` (hex, lowercase). Add a width and a `0` prefix to pad with leading zeros:

```rust
let n: u8 = 13;
format!("{:08b}", n);  // "00001101"  - 8 digits, zero-padded
format!("{:02x}", n);  // "0d"        - 2 digits, zero-padded
```

You can reuse the same value across multiple `{...}` placeholders, so the full one-liner is:

```rust
format!("{} = 0b{:08b} = 0x{:02x}", x, x, x)
```

The `0b` and `0x` prefixes in the output are **literal text**, not part of the format spec — Rust prints them verbatim because you wrote them between the braces.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 - popcount in one call"}
[`u32::count_ones`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u32.html#method.count_ones) returns the number of 1-bits in a `u32`. It compiles to a single `popcnt` instruction on x86-64 and a single `cnt` instruction on AArch64. There is also [`u32::count_zeros`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u32.html#method.count_zeros) (you do not need it here), and an equivalent method on every other integer type ([`u64::count_ones`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u64.html#method.count_ones), [`u8::count_ones`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html#method.count_ones), and so on).

```rust
fn bit_count(x: u32) -> u32 {
    x.count_ones()
}
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 - Setting and clearing a single bit"}
To **set** bit `i` (force it to 1) without touching the other bits, OR with a mask that has just that bit set:

```rust
let mask = 1u8 << i;   // e.g. i=3 -> 0b0000_1000
let new  = x | mask;
```

To **clear** bit `i` (force it to 0) without touching the other bits, AND with the *complement* of that mask (every bit set *except* bit `i`):

```rust
let mask = 1u8 << i;
let new  = x & !mask;  // ! flips every bit
```

This pattern — "build a 1-bit mask, then OR to set or AND-NOT to clear" — is the foundation of every bitset, every bitfield, and every flag register in systems programming.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn show_three_bases(x: u8) -> String {
    format!("{} = 0b{:08b} = 0x{:02x}", x, x, x)
}

fn bit_count(x: u32) -> u32 {
    x.count_ones()
}

fn three_ops(a: u8, b: u8) -> (u8, u8, u8) {
    (a & b, a | b, a ^ b)
}

fn set_bit(x: u8, i: u8) -> u8 {
    x | (1 << i)
}

fn clear_bit(x: u8, i: u8) -> u8 {
    x & !(1 << i)
}
```

The full reference implementation lives in `day13/ex-numbers-and-bits-solution/src/main.rs`. Try not to peek before your own version passes all six tests.
:::

## Where this leads

- **2-bit DNA packing.** Exercise 2 uses left-shift and OR to fit a 32-base DNA sequence into a single `u64`. The base at index `i` lives in bits `(2*i)..(2*i+2)` — exactly the shift-and-mask pattern from `set_bit` here, but with 2-bit slots instead of 1-bit slots.
- **Hamming distance in two instructions.** Exercise 3 combines XOR (one instruction, finds the mismatched bits) with [`u64::count_ones`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u64.html#method.count_ones) (one instruction, counts the mismatched bits) to compute Hamming distance on two 32-base sequences faster than any byte-by-byte loop could.
- **Barcode whitelists.** The same XOR + popcount trick underlies fast single-cell barcode matching in [Cell Ranger](https://www.10xgenomics.com/support/software/cell-ranger), [STARsolo](https://github.com/alexdobin/STAR), and [salmon alevin](https://salmon.readthedocs.io/). You will build a tiny version of that in `ex-barcode-whitelist`.
